likely_questions: What is the correlation between study hours and exam scores?
How do students' programming skills relate to their overall performance?
What is the average age of students in the program?
domain_details.reasoning: The presence of columns such as 'entryEXAM', 'prevEducation', and 'studyHOURS' suggests a focus on student performance and academic metrics. The inclusion of programming skills ('Python' and 'DB') further supports this interpretation.

quality_summary:
Your data has a quality score of 87.6/100. Here's what we found:
• Some entries are missing in columns like: Python
• Some unusual values were found in: Age, entryEXAM

reason_data.why: Column 'Python' had 2 missing values (2.6% of 77 rows) — any calculation using this column would silently ignore those rows.
reason_data.risk: Without this fix, totals, averages, and comparisons on 'Python' would be based on only 75 rows instead of 77, giving misleadingly low figures.
reason_data.method_why: The average value of 'Python' was used to fill gaps, which is appropriate when the data has no extreme outliers that would distort the average. The value used was: 75.85.

reason_data.why: Column 'Python' contained numbers but was stored as text. For example, '1,250' or ' 99.5 ' are text values — the system cannot add, average, or compare them until they are converted to actual numbers.
reason_data.risk: Without this fix, any formula or calculation on 'Python' would either fail with an error or silently produce wrong results. Sorting would sort alphabetically ('9' before '10') instead of numerically.
reason_data.method_why: Each value in 'Python' was parsed and converted to a number. Values that could not be converted (e.g. text like 'N/A') were set to missing and handled by the fill step.

reason_data.why: Column 'Age' had 2 extreme values (2.6% of rows) (actual data range: 21.00 to 71.00) that fell far outside the normal range for this data. The expected normal range for 'Age' is 4.50 to 64.50 (calculated using the interquartile range method).
reason_data.risk: Extreme values in 'Age' would heavily distort averages and totals. For instance, a single data entry error of 1,000,000 in a salary column can move the company-wide average salary by thousands of pounds, leading to wrong budget decisions.
reason_data.method_why: Extreme values were capped to the range 0.00 – 100.00 rather than removed entirely. Capping (also called Winsorising) keeps the row in the dataset — only the single extreme value is adjusted to the boundary. This preserves the data point while eliminating its distorting effect.

tiles.title: Avg Age by fNAME
tiles.insight: Perry has the highest average age at 71.00, while Morten has the lowest at 46.00, with a significant 25-year gap between them. This large age difference may indicate different student demographics, which could be worth exploring further.

tiles.title: Age distribution
tiles.insight: Most students are in their early twenties, with 36% of the records falling in that age range. A small number of older students, mostly in their fifties and sixties, make up the majority of the data, indicating a skewed distribution.

tiles.title: fNAME breakdown
tiles.insight: The majority of fNAME records are evenly distributed across 7 groups, with the top group accounting for only 14% of records. This balanced distribution suggests that the fNAME data is relatively consistent across different groups in the education domain.

tiles.title: Correlation with Age
tiles.insight: Study hours are weakly linked to age, suggesting that students who study more tend to be a bit older. However, the relationships between age and other factors like entry exam scores and programming skills are relatively weak, indicating these columns don't strongly influence each other.
